definition: The quantity of a syllable is the relative amount of time taken to pronounce it
notes: defined after first use

definition: the accent (stress) of a word; words of two syllables have it on the first, polysyllables on the penult when long, otherwise on the antepenult
notes: Pharr glosses accent as "stress" (§25) and otherwise states only its placement rules

definition: A diphthong is the union of two vowels in a single syllable

A syllable is a single uninterrupted unit of sound in a spoken word, built around one vowel or diphthong. A Latin word has as many syllables as it has vowels and diphthongs, so amō has two (a-mō) and patria has three (pa-tri-a). Syllables matter in Latin because both the position of the accent and the rhythm of poetry are measured in them.
notes: Pharr gives only a counting rule (§9: a word has as many syllables as it has vowels and diphthongs), never a definition of what a syllable is
in_situ_rationale: familiarity: students use 'syllable' fluently in English; Pharr's surrounding counting rule makes the Latin application self-evident

definition: A vowel is a speech sound made with the breath flowing freely, with no blocking of the air by the tongue, teeth, or lips. In Latin the vowels are a, e, i, o, u (and y in Greek words). Every syllable is built around a vowel or a diphthong, and a vowel can be short or long — a length that Latin poetry measures.
in_situ_rationale: familiarity: known from English; §6 contrasts vowels with consonants by example

definition: A consonant is a speech sound made by blocking or narrowing the breath somewhere in the mouth with the tongue, teeth, or lips — sounds like b, t, m, or s. Consonants frame the vowel at the heart of each syllable. In Latin a doubled consonant is pronounced twice, and a vowel followed by two or more consonants usually makes its syllable count as long for poetry.
notes: used but not defined by Pharr
in_situ_rationale: familiarity: known from English; §7's doubled-consonant examples make the Latin point clear

definition: A syllable is long by nature when it contains a long vowel or a diphthong
A syllable is long by position when its vowel is followed by two or more consonants pronounced separately, or by either double consonant x or z
the slightly greater stress given to the first (long) syllable of each foot in a verse
notes: Pharr defines this only in the context of the hexameter (§394a: "this stress is the metrical accent")

definition: If a consonant followed by l or r comes after a short vowel, the syllable with the short vowel is said to be common — it may be either long or short, at the speaker’s pleasure

Case is the form a noun, pronoun, or adjective takes to show its job in the sentence — whether it is the subject, the direct object, a possessor, and so on. Latin words change their endings to mark case, and Latin has seven: nominative, genitive, dative, accusative, ablative, vocative, and locative.
notes: reclassified Pharr->editor in glossary cleanup Phase 2: Pharr's §30 entry was an enumerative definition — a bare count that never says what case is; rewritten as a real definition with the seven cases kept as a trailing aid. Original Pharr definition: 'Latin has seven cases'.

definition: The last syllable of a word is the ultima
the next-to-last syllable is the penult
the syllable before the penult is the antepenult

A question is a sentence that asks for information rather than stating a fact. Latin often marks a yes-or-no question by attaching the enclitic -ne to the first word, or uses a question word such as quis 'who?' or cūr 'why?'
in_situ_rationale: familiarity / salience: in §27 'question' appears only in passing to explain the enclitic -ne; students know what a question is

definition: Voice shows the relationship between the subject of a verb and its action. In the active voice the subject does the action (parō 'I prepare'); in the passive voice the subject receives it (paror 'I am prepared'). Latin also has traces of a middle voice, passive in form, in which the subject acts on or for itself.
notes: first_appearance corrected from §28 (non-technical "pronunciation" sense) to §115 (first grammatical use); Pharr defines the three voices at §115a but not "voice" itself
in_situ_rationale: umbrella: Pharr defines the three voices at §115a, which carry the reading; the general notion is not salient enough in situ to justify the obtrusion

definition: Number is the grammatical property that shows whether a word refers to one thing or to more than one. Latin distinguishes two numbers, the singular (one) and the plural (more than one); nouns, pronouns, adjectives, and verbs all change form to mark it.
notes: enumerative definition; reclassified Pharr->editor in Thread 2: Pharr's §29 is an enumerative definition ('Latin has two numbers...'), not a definition of 'number'; given the umbrella treatment like voice/mood
in_situ_rationale: umbrella: Pharr's §29 enumerates the two numbers (singular, plural), which are transparent and carry the reading; the general property is glossary material, not salient enough in situ

definition: denoting one
denoting more than one

A preposition is a small word placed before a noun or pronoun to show its relationship to the rest of the sentence — relations of place, time, or direction (in 'in/into', ad 'to', cum 'with', sine 'without'). In Latin each preposition requires its noun in a particular case, either the accusative or the ablative.
in_situ_note: little words placed before a noun to show a relation such as place or direction, like English 'to' or 'with'
in_situ_rationale: keep, appositive: the Latin-specific case-government point is left to §30's context; the note gives the core meaning with one or two English examples, the Latin examples being available on the page || Thread 3: moved from §30 to §312. §30's mention is the English preposition 'of' inside the genitive definition; §312 ('used with many prepositions') is the first meaningful Latin-grammar context. Pluralized to agree with the anchor noun.

definition: the case of the subject
usually equivalent to the English possessive, or to the objective with the preposition of
the case of the indirect object or of personal interest
usually the case of the direct object
the case of adverbial relation
the case of direct address
the case of the place where

The subject is the person or thing a sentence is about — the one doing the action, or being described. In Latin the subject normally stands in the nominative case, and the verb changes its ending to match the subject in person and number.
in_situ_note: the person or thing the sentence is about, usually the doer of the action
in_situ_rationale: keep, meaning-only: students half-know 'subject', but their grammar gaps make the nominative link worth stating; nominative is glossed at §30 immediately above || Thread 3: kept at §30 (core to the Nominative's identity, unlike the intrusive prepositional note); placed as an appositive in the Nominative cell.

definition: An object is a person or thing that the action of the verb is done to or directed toward. The [[direct object]] receives the action and stands in the accusative case; the [[indirect object]] is the one to or for whom something is done and stands in the dative case.
in_situ_note: the person or thing the verb's action is done to or directed toward
in_situ_rationale: keep, meaning-only: the core meaning is what the student needs; the accusative/dative split is laid out in §30 just above, so the note leaves it to context || Thread 3: kept at §30 (core to the Accusative's identity); placed as an appositive in the Accusative cell.

definition: A declension is one of the classes into which Latin nouns are grouped according to how they form their endings; all the nouns in a declension share the same set of case endings. There are five declensions, told apart by the final letter of the noun's stem (the stem characteristic).
notes: reclassified Pharr->editor in glossary cleanup Phase 2: Pharr's §31 entry under-defined the term, giving the count and the distinguishing feature without saying a declension is a class of nouns sharing one set of endings; rewritten as a real definition keeping the count and stem-characteristic detail. Original Pharr definition: 'There are five declensions in Latin, characterized by the final letter of their stems (the stem characteristic)'.

definition: The consonant declensions are Pharr's grouping of the third and fourth declensions together, because their stems end in a consonant (or behave as if they do) rather than in a distinct vowel. He contrasts them with the vowel declensions (first, second, and fifth). The label is an organizing convenience, not a separate declension to memorize.
in_situ_rationale: member-of-a-class / salience: §31 itself names and contrasts vowel vs consonant declensions in the same breath, supplying the schema that makes the grouping self-evident

definition: A word's stem is the core part that stays the same while endings are added to it. You find a noun's stem by taking the genitive singular and removing its ending; to that stem the other case endings attach. Verbs likewise have stems to which personal endings are added. The stem's final letter — the stem characteristic — sorts nouns into the five declensions.
notes: used throughout but never defined as such; §31 introduces "stem characteristic", §116 describes how the verb stem is modified
in_situ_note: the unchanging cores of words, to which endings are attached
in_situ_rationale: keep, meaning-only: load-bearing term used everywhere after §31; §31 supplies the genitive recipe and the stem-characteristic application || Thread 3: anchored at §31 ('the final letter of their stems'); pluralized to 'cores of words' to read as an appositive to plural 'stems'.

definition: the final letter of a stem, by which the declensions (and conjugations) are characterized
notes: introduced in passing

definition: Neuter is one of the three Latin genders, used for many (though not all) nouns naming things rather than people (bellum 'war', dōnum 'gift'). Neuter nouns have one special rule: their nominative, accusative, and vocative are always identical, and in the plural these always end in -a.
in_situ_note: the third Latin gender
in_situ_rationale: keep, meaning-only: unlike masculine/feminine, neuter carries a distinctive morphological rule (three cases alike, plural -a) that a student needs flagged where neuters first appear || Thread 3: anchored at §33c ('Neuter nouns'); note trimmed to a true appositive ('the third Latin gender') since §33c itself already states the nom/acc/voc-alike and -a plural facts.

definition: The second declension is the family of Latin nouns whose stem ends in -o (usually appearing as -u-). Its nouns are mostly masculine or neuter; the masculines have nominative singular in -us or -er, the neuters in -um.
in_situ_rationale: member-of-a-class: located by the §31 schema; §36 supplies its own stem and ending details

definition: The first declension is the first of Latin's five noun families, made up of nouns whose stem ends in -ā. Its nouns are mostly feminine, and the nominative singular ends in -a (terra 'land', puella 'girl'). Each declension shares one set of case endings.
in_situ_rationale: member-of-a-class: §31 lays out what a declension is and the differentiating axis (stem characteristic + genitive) for all five, so the member is salient from the parent schema

definition: Gender is the grammatical class of a noun — in Latin, masculine, feminine, or neuter. It is a property of the word, not a claim about biological sex: many things with no sex at all have a gender (mēnsa 'table' is feminine, bellum 'war' is neuter). A noun's gender controls the form of any adjective or pronoun that agrees with it.
in_situ_rationale: umbrella: §35 introduces gender together with its three values, which are transparent from their names and do the interpretive work

definition: Feminine is one of the three Latin genders. Nouns naming female beings are feminine (fēmina 'woman', rēgīna 'queen'), and so are most first-declension nouns and many others, by grammatical convention rather than meaning. A noun's gender determines the feminine form an adjective takes when it agrees with the noun.
in_situ_rationale: member-of-a-class / salience: parallel to masculine; established with the other genders at §35 and clear from its name

definition: Masculine is one of the three Latin genders. Nouns naming male beings are masculine (vir 'man', rēx 'king'), but so are very many nouns for things and ideas, by grammatical convention rather than meaning. A noun's gender determines the masculine form an adjective takes when it agrees with the noun.
in_situ_rationale: member-of-a-class / salience: §35 establishes the three genders together; the value is transparent from its English name

definition: A mute (also called a stop) is a consonant made by briefly stopping the breath completely and then releasing it: b, p, d, t, g, c. Pharr groups the third-declension consonant stems by their final letter, and the mutes form one such group. The term matters mainly for one rhythm rule: a mute followed by l or r can leave a preceding short syllable free to count as either long or short in poetry.
in_situ_note: mutes being consonants made by briefly stopping the breath completely before releasing it: b, p, d, t, g, c
in_situ_rationale: keep, meaning-only: arcane label students will not know; the term's meaning is what they lack, since §39 supplies its application to the consonant stems || Thread 3: anchored to the 'Mute stems' label in the §39 classification list; reworded as an appositive ('mutes being …'). || Thread 3 revision: inline gloss in the §39 classification list broke the brace/row layout. Replaced by a single editorial 'Consonant types' box (.edbox) inserted between the consonant-stems list and the i-Stems heading, which defines mutes, liquids, and sibilants together. No standalone .ed gloss for this term.

definition: A liquid is one of the consonants l, m, n, r, whose sound flows smoothly and can be held like a vowel. Pharr uses the term to name one group of third-declension consonant stems — the liquid stems, whose stem ends in l, m, n, or r.
in_situ_note: liquids being the consonants l, m, n, r, whose sound flows smoothly and can be drawn out
in_situ_rationale: keep, meaning-only: unfamiliar technical label; the meaning is what the student needs, since §39 supplies its role in the stem classification || Thread 3: anchored to the 'Liquid stems' label in the §39 classification list; reworded as an appositive ('liquids being …'). || Thread 3 revision: inline gloss in the §39 classification list broke the brace/row layout. Replaced by a single editorial 'Consonant types' box (.edbox) inserted between the consonant-stems list and the i-Stems heading, which defines mutes, liquids, and sibilants together. No standalone .ed gloss for this term.

definition: A sibilant is a fricative with a sharp hissing quality, produced by forcing a continuous stream of air past the teeth or the ridge behind them. Fricatives more generally are consonants made by narrowing the vocal tract enough to create audible friction without fully stopping the breath (unlike the mutes, which close it completely); Latin f is a fricative but not a sibilant, since it is formed at the lip and teeth and lacks the hiss. The only true sibilant in Latin is s.
notes: Added in Thread 3 at Sam's request to complete the trio of consonant types named in the §39 classification list (liquid, sibilant, mute). Pharr names 'Sibilant stems' but does not define the term. Definition revised to situate sibilant within the broader fricative category and to note that f is a fricative but not a sibilant; the earlier x/z remark was dropped as inaccurate (x is the cluster /ks/; z is a Greek borrowing).
in_situ_rationale: Thread 3: covered, with mutes and liquids, by the editorial 'Consonant types' box (.edbox) inserted between the consonant-stems list and the i-Stems heading in §39, rather than as a standalone .ed gloss. The fuller definition (including rhotacism) lives here in the database for later glossary work.

definition: The third declension is the largest and most varied family of Latin nouns, made up of those whose stem ends in a consonant or in -i. Its nouns come in all three genders, and the nominative singular takes many shapes, so this declension is recognized by its genitive singular ending -is rather than by its nominative.
in_situ_rationale: member-of-a-class: §31 supplies the differentiating schema and §39 the local stem breakdown, so the member is salient from context rather than needing reglossing

definition: A foot is the basic rhythmic unit of a line of verse — a small fixed pattern of long and short syllables that repeats to make up the line. The verse of the Aeneid is built from six feet, each a dactyl (one long followed by two shorts) or a spondee (two longs). Identifying these feet is what scansion does.
notes: used only within the hexameter scheme (§392–409); Pharr never defines the metrical foot as such. §392 description ("each foot opens with a long syllable bearing the metrical accent") is true only of dactylic/spondaic feet, not feet in general
in_situ_note: a foot being the basic rhythmic unit of a verse line, a set pattern of long and short syllables
in_situ_rationale: keep, meaning-only: salient and central to the metrical section; dactyl/spondee are explained right at §392-394, so the note gives only the core meaning || Thread 3: moved from §52 (a t/d-stem noun paradigm, unrelated) to §392 ('six feet in a hexameter verse'), the first metrical-foot context; reworded as an appositive.

definition: An i-stem is a third-declension noun whose stem originally ended in -i rather than a plain consonant. Traces of that -i survive in a few endings — most noticeably the genitive plural in -ium instead of -um — which is what sets i-stems apart from ordinary consonant stems.
in_situ_note: third-declension nouns whose stem ends in -i rather than a plain consonant
in_situ_rationale: keep, meaning-only: unlike the numbered declensions, §39–40 describe how i-stems decline but do not foreground the diagnostic that tells one apart, so the meaning still earns a note; 'third declension' is in the immediate vicinity || Thread 3: anchored at §81 ('i-stem nouns'), the first discursive use, rather than the bare §56 table stub; pluralized appositive.

definition: The fourth declension is a small family of Latin nouns whose stem ends in -u, with nominative singular in -us (mostly masculine) or -ū (a few neuters). Its endings closely resemble those of the third declension.
in_situ_rationale: member-of-a-class: located by the §31 schema; §58 supplies its own characteristics

definition: The fifth declension is the smallest family of Latin nouns, with stems ending in -ē and nominative singular in -ēs (diēs 'day', rēs 'thing'). Its nouns are mostly feminine, the chief exception being diēs.
in_situ_rationale: member-of-a-class: located by the §31 schema; §62 supplies its own characteristics

definition: A noun is a word that names a person, place, thing, or idea (puella 'girl', urbs 'city', virtūs 'courage'). In Latin a noun changes its ending to show its case, number, and gender, and those endings are organized into the five declensions.
notes: used but not defined by Pharr
in_situ_rationale: familiarity: 'noun' is firmly known from English; §70 uses it only to say adjectives are declined like nouns

definition: The adjective adds a quality to its noun or pronoun
A Pronoun is a word that may be substituted for a noun
notes: defined after first use

definition: A pronominal adjective is an adjective that declines partly like a pronoun rather than like an ordinary adjective — most visibly with a genitive singular in -īus and a dative singular in -ī for all genders. This small group includes ūnus 'one', sōlus 'alone', tōtus 'whole', alter 'the other', and nūllus 'none.'
in_situ_note: so called because they decline partly like a pronoun
in_situ_rationale: keep, appositive: §73 lists the members and the -īus/-ī endings right there, so the note need only say why the name 'pronominal' is used || Thread 3: inline appositive after the existing .term anchor in §73.

definition: The Present denotes continuance in the present: (1) of what is going on now; (2) of statements true for all time

Comparison is the way an adjective or adverb changes its form to show degrees of a quality. Latin distinguishes three degrees: the positive states the quality plainly (altus 'high'), the comparative shows a greater degree of it (altior 'higher'), and the superlative shows the greatest (altissimus 'highest').
in_situ_rationale: umbrella: §83 immediately names its three subtypes, each glossed nearby; like mood, the general notion is not salient enough in situ to justify the obtrusion

definition: The positive is the basic, uncompared degree of an adjective or adverb — the quality stated plainly, with no notion of 'more' or 'most' (altus 'high', clārus 'clear'). It is the dictionary form and the base from which the comparative and superlative are built.
in_situ_rationale: member-of-a-class / salience: §83-84 present the three degrees together; once comparison is in view, the positive as the plain base form is self-evident

definition: The comparative is the degree of an adjective or adverb that expresses a greater amount of a quality — English '-er' or 'more.' It compares two things (altior 'higher'), or means 'rather/too' when nothing is compared with it. In Latin it is formed by adding -ior (masculine and feminine) and -ius (neuter) to the stem.
notes: Pharr gives formation only (§84: "is formed by adding…"), no statement of meaning; definition is yours to write
in_situ_note: the degree of an adjective expressing a greater amount of a quality, English '-er' or 'more', used in comparing two things
in_situ_rationale: keep, meaning-only: Pharr's §84 gives the -ior/-ius formation, so the note supplies only what the comparative is, letting the student interpret his rule || Thread 3: inline appositive after the existing .term anchor in §84; rephrased as a parenthetical appositive.

definition: The superlative is the degree of an adjective or adverb that expresses the greatest amount of a quality — English '-est' or 'most.' It singles out one from a group (altissimus 'highest'), or means 'very' when nothing is compared. In Latin it is usually formed by adding -issimus to the stem.
notes: Pharr gives formation only (§85: "is formed by adding…"), no statement of meaning; definition is yours to write
in_situ_note: the degree of an adjective expressing the greatest amount of a quality, English '-est' or 'most'
in_situ_rationale: keep, meaning-only: Pharr's §85 gives the -issimus formation; the note supplies only what the superlative is || Thread 3: moved from §84 to §85, where the 'Superlative' .term anchor actually is; inline appositive after it.

definition: Adverbs qualify verbs, adjectives, and other adverbs
The Cardinal Numerals, as ūnus one, duo two, decem ten, answer the question quot how many? and are the numbers used in counting
notes: Pharr definition at §98 missed in extraction

definition: The Ordinal Numerals, as prīmus first, secundus (alter) second, decimus tenth, answer the question quotus which one in the series?
notes: Pharr definition at §98a missed in extraction

definition: The Distributive Numerals, as singulī one each, bīnī two each, dēnī ten each, answer the question quotēnī how many each?
notes: Pharr definition at §100 missed in extraction

definition: Numeral Adverbs, as semel once, bis twice, ter thrice, answer the question quotiēns how often?
notes: Pharr definition at §101 missed in extraction

definition: A reflexive pronoun refers back to the subject of its own clause — it points to the same person as the doer of the action (English 'myself', 'himself', 'themselves'). Latin uses the personal pronouns for this in the first and second persons, and a special pronoun suī, sibī, sē for the third person.
notes: used but not defined by Pharr
in_situ_note: A reflexive pronoun refers back to the subject of its own clause — 'himself', 'herself', 'themselves'.
in_situ_rationale: keep, meaning-only: the 'refers back to the subject' idea is the need; subject is recoverable (§30) and pronoun is Pharr-defined at §102 just before; the suī/sibī/sē forms left to context || Thread 3: anchored at §106 title ('The Personal (Reflexive) Pronoun'), the first dedicated reflexive context (not the bare §102 list); worked into a full sentence after the section title.

definition: A personal pronoun is a pronoun that stands for a particular person by grammatical person: the speaker (ego 'I', nōs 'we'), the one addressed (tū 'you', vōs 'you'), or the one spoken about. Because a Latin verb already shows its person in the ending, the nominative personal pronouns are used mainly for emphasis or contrast.
in_situ_note: A personal pronoun stands for a particular person — 'I', 'you', 'we'; in Latin the nominative forms are used mainly for emphasis.
in_situ_rationale: keep, meaning-only: the emphasis point is the Latin-specific need; person is introduced in this vicinity (§103) || Thread 3: anchored at §103 ('Personal Pronoun of the First Person'); worked into a full sentence after the title.

definition: Person shows whether the subject of a verb is the speaker, the one spoken to, or someone else: the first person is the speaker (I, we), the second the one addressed (you), the third anyone or anything else (he, she, it, they). A Latin verb shows its person through its ending.
in_situ_rationale: umbrella: §115/§103 name first/second/third right there, transparent from their English pronouns; the subtypes carry the reading

definition: A possessive adjective shows who or what something belongs to, agreeing like an ordinary adjective with the thing possessed, not the possessor (meus 'my', tuus 'your', noster 'our', suus 'his/her/their own'). So in liber meus, 'my book,' meus is masculine to match liber.
notes: used but not defined by Pharr
in_situ_note: adjectives showing ownership and agreeing with the thing owned, not the owner — 'my', 'your', 'his/her/their own'
in_situ_rationale: keep, meaning-only: the agreement-with-the-thing-owned point heads off a common error; adjective is Pharr-defined at §70 || Thread 3: moved to §102b ('The possessive adjectives are often classified …'), the first meaningful occurrence, earlier than §105; pluralized appositive.

definition: A demonstrative pronoun points out a particular person or thing — English 'this', 'that', 'these', 'those.' Latin's chief demonstratives are hic 'this', ille 'that', and is 'this/that' (the ordinary unemphatic one); each may also be used as an adjective with a noun.
in_situ_note: A demonstrative pronoun points something out — 'this', 'that', 'these', 'those'.
in_situ_rationale: keep, meaning-only: the 'this/that' meaning is the need; the hic/ille/is forms and adjective use left to the §106 vicinity || Thread 3: moved from §106 (reflexive) to the §107 demonstrative section; added as a full intro sentence after the 'B. Demonstrative Pronouns' heading.

definition: An intensive pronoun emphasizes a noun or pronoun, singling it out as the very one meant — English 'himself', 'itself', 'the very' (in the sense of emphasis, not reflexive reference). Latin uses ipse, ipsa, ipsum.
in_situ_note: An intensive pronoun emphasizes a noun or pronoun, singling it out as the very one meant — 'himself', 'the very'; this is emphasis, not the reflexive's reference back to the subject.
in_situ_rationale: keep, meaning-only: the emphasis sense and the contrast with the reflexive are the need; reflexive appears just before (§102, §106), so the contrast is recoverable; ipse forms left to context || Thread 3: anchored at the §108 'C. The Intensive Pronoun' heading; added as a full intro sentence (heading-anchored dedicated section).

definition: A relative pronoun introduces a clause that describes a noun in the main sentence, linking back to that noun (its antecedent) — English 'who', 'which', 'that.' Latin uses quī, quae, quod, which agrees with its antecedent in gender and number but takes its case from its own role in the relative clause.
in_situ_note: A relative pronoun introduces a clause that describes an earlier noun (its antecedent) — 'who', 'which', 'that'.
in_situ_rationale: keep, meaning-only: the antecedent-linking meaning is the need; the agreement-but-own-case rule and quī/quae/quod forms left to §109's vicinity; gender/number/case are foundational || Thread 3: anchored at the §109 'D. The Relative Pronoun' heading; added as a full intro sentence.

definition: An interrogative pronoun asks a question about a person or thing — English 'who?', 'what?', 'which?' Latin uses quis? 'who?' and quid? 'what?' (and the adjective quī? quae? quod? 'which?'). It is identical in form to the relative pronoun, told apart by its questioning function.
in_situ_note: An interrogative pronoun asks a question about a person or thing — 'who?', 'what?', 'which?'.
in_situ_rationale: keep, meaning-only: the questioning function is the need and is what distinguishes it from the look-alike relative pronoun, which appears just before at §109; quis/quid forms left to context || Thread 3: moved from §110 ('General Relatives') to the §110-area 'E. The Interrogative Pronoun' heading; added as a full intro sentence.

definition: A verb is a word that expresses an action or a state of being (vocat 'calls', est 'is'). A Latin verb is highly inflected: its endings show person, number, tense, mood, and voice at once, so a single word like amābam already means 'I was loving.'
in_situ_rationale: familiarity: 'verb' is well known from English; §115 immediately proceeds to spell out person, voice, mood, etc.

definition: The inflection given to the verbal stem is called conjugation, and expresses person, voice, mood, tense, and number
The active voice denotes that the action proceeds from the subject: parō I prepare
notes: denotative definition (Pharr defines by function)

definition: The passive voice denotes that the subject receives the action: paror I am prepared
notes: denotative definition (Pharr defines by function)

definition: The middle voice has passive form and denotes the subject as acting for or upon itself (reflexive): paror I prepare (for) myself
notes: denotative definition (Pharr defines by function)

definition: Mood is the form of a verb that shows how the speaker regards the action — as a fact, a command, or something merely possible or imagined. Latin has three: the indicative (states a fact or asks a question), the imperative (gives a command), and the subjunctive (expresses a wish, possibility, or other non-factual idea).
notes: Pharr defines the three moods at §115 (4a) but not "mood" itself
in_situ_rationale: umbrella: Pharr defines the three moods at §115 (4a), which carry the reading; like voice, mood-in-general is not salient enough in situ to justify the obtrusion

definition: The indicative is the mood of fact: parō I prepare
The subjunctive is the mood of wish, command, or qualified statement: parem may I prepare, I may prepare ; paret may he prepare, let him prepare ; sī paret if he should prepare
The imperative is the mood of command: parā prepare!

Tense is the form of a verb that shows the time of its action — present, past, or future — and whether the action is going on, finished, or yet to be completed. Latin has six tenses: present, imperfect, future, perfect, pluperfect, and future perfect.
notes: reclassified Pharr->editor in glossary cleanup Phase 2: Pharr's §351 entry was an enumerative definition that lists the six tenses without saying what tense is; rewritten as a real definition with the enumeration kept as a trailing aid. Original Pharr definition: 'There are six tenses in Latin: present, imperfect, future, perfect, pluperfect, and future perfect'.

definition: The Imperfect denotes continued or repeated action in the past: pugnābam , I was fighting, I kept fighting, I used to fight
notes: denotative definition (Pharr defines by function)

definition: The Future denotes continuance in the future, and also indefinite future action
The Pure Perfect (present perfect) expresses completion in the present, of an action now over; the Historical (aoristic) Perfect denotes a single act in past time
The Pluperfect denotes completion in the past, of an action completed before another began: fuerat inimīcus , he had been my enemy
notes: denotative definition (Pharr defines by function)

definition: The Future Perfect denotes both completion and attainment: fēcerō , I shall have done it, or I shall do it (once and for all)
notes: denotative definition (Pharr defines by function)

definition: stem of the present, imperfect, and future tenses (the present system)
notes: reclassified editor->Pharr in Thread 2: Pharr defines this at §116

definition: stem of the perfect, pluperfect, and future perfect active (the perfect system)
notes: reclassified editor->Pharr in Thread 2: Pharr defines this at §116

definition: used to form the perfect, pluperfect, and future perfect passive, and also the future active and perfect passive participles
notes: reclassified editor->Pharr in Thread 2: Pharr defines this at §116

definition: The finite forms of a verb are those limited by a personal ending, which fixes the action to a particular person and number (first, second, or third; singular or plural) and carries a tense, mood, and voice. A finite verb has a built-in subject: vocat, 'he calls.' These are the forms displayed in the conjugation tables. They are opposed to the infinite forms (the verbal nouns and adjectives), which have no personal ending.
notes: Pharr does not use the term 'finite' but the conjugation he sets out from §115 is the finite system, opposed to his 'infinite parts of the verb' (§117); added in Thread 2
in_situ_rationale: umbrella/box: in-situ treatment provided by the orienting box 'Finite and Infinite Forms of the Verb', to be placed after §115 in the HTML

variants: infinite; infinite parts of the verb; infinitives (in Pharr's broad sense)
definition: The infinite forms of a verb — Pharr's 'infinite parts of the verb' (§117) — are those that lack a personal ending and so name no subject of their own ('infinite' here meaning unbounded by person, not endless). Each turns the verb into a noun or an adjective while keeping its sense of action, tense, and voice. They are five: the infinitive, gerund, and supine (verbal nouns) and the participle and gerundive (verbal adjectives).
notes: Pharr's term is 'the infinite parts of the verb' (§117); comprises infinitive, gerund, supine, participle, gerundive; added in Thread 2 | BOX TEXT (for HTML insertion after §115): FINITE AND INFINITE FORMS OF THE VERB | A Latin verb has two broad kinds of forms. FINITE forms are limited (“finite”) by a personal ending: they fix the action to a person and number (I, you, he; we, you, they) and carry a tense, mood, and voice. Vocat, “he calls,” is finite — it has a built-in subject. These are the forms set out in the conjugation tables that follow. | The INFINITE forms (Pharr’s “infinite parts of the verb,” §117) are not limited in that way — “infinite” here means unbounded by person. Having no personal ending, they name no subject of their own. Instead each turns the verb into a noun or an adjective, while keeping the verb’s sense of action, tense, and voice. There are five: | A verb can become a NOUN in three ways. The INFINITIVE names the action as a thing — English “to” plus a verb (amāre, “to love”). The GERUND is the action treated as a declinable noun, like the English “-ing” noun in “the art of loving” (amandī, “of loving”). The SUPINE is a rare noun surviving in just two forms, used to express purpose (-um) or respect (-ū). | A verb can become an ADJECTIVE in two ways. The PARTICIPLE describes a noun while keeping the verb’s action and time — a verbal adjective (amāns, “loving”; amātus, “having been loved”). The GERUNDIVE is a verbal adjective of obligation — what ought to be done (amandus, “to be loved”). | Each of these is built on one of the three stems given in §116, and each agrees or is used according to its kind: the noun-forms take cases, the adjective-forms agree with a noun in gender, number, and case.
in_situ_rationale: umbrella/box: in-situ treatment provided by the orienting box 'Finite and Infinite Forms of the Verb', to be placed after §115 in the HTML

definition: A participle is a verbal adjective — a form built from a verb that describes a noun while keeping a sense of action and time. Latin has participles in several tenses and voices (present active amāns 'loving', perfect passive amātus 'having been loved', future active amātūrus 'about to love'). Like any adjective, a participle agrees with its noun in gender, number, and case.
notes: in-situ note deferred: these infinite verb-forms await a unified orienting treatment of the finite/infinite distinction (Pharr's typology, §116–117) and the place of the verbal nouns/adjectives within it, to be inserted at the start of the Conjugation section in the HTML; a per-term in-situ gloss at the shared §116/§117 anchor is awkward and would duplicate that treatment
in_situ_rationale: deferred to planned §116 finite/infinite treatment (see notes)

definition: The infinitive is a verbal noun — the form of a verb that names its action as a thing, without a personal subject (English 'to' plus a verb: amāre 'to love'). In Latin it is used only in the nominative and accusative, carries tense and voice (present amāre, present passive amārī, perfect amāvisse), and is central to constructions such as indirect statement. It is not the same as the verb's root or stem.
notes: used but not defined by Pharr; first appearance is §116 (in the finite/infinite framing), not §117; earlier definition wrongly equated the infinitive with the verb's basic/unconjugated form (the root); in-situ note deferred: these infinite verb-forms await a unified orienting treatment of the finite/infinite distinction (Pharr's typology, §116–117) and the place of the verbal nouns/adjectives within it, to be inserted at the start of the Conjugation section in the HTML; a per-term in-situ gloss at the shared §116/§117 anchor is awkward and would duplicate that treatment
in_situ_rationale: deferred to planned §116 treatment (see notes); the in-situ note does not fit at the shared first appearance, and the earlier 'basic unconjugated form' gloss was wrong — that describes the root, not the infinitive

definition: The gerund is a verbal noun — a form that turns the action of a verb into a noun you can decline, much like English '-ing' used as a noun ('the art of loving'). Latin's gerund appears in the genitive, dative, accusative, and ablative; it has no nominative, the infinitive serving in its place.
notes: in-situ note deferred: these infinite verb-forms await a unified orienting treatment of the finite/infinite distinction (Pharr's typology, §116–117) and the place of the verbal nouns/adjectives within it, to be inserted at the start of the Conjugation section in the HTML; a per-term in-situ gloss at the shared §116/§117 anchor is awkward and would duplicate that treatment
in_situ_rationale: deferred to planned §116 finite/infinite treatment (see notes)

definition: The gerundive is a verbal adjective expressing necessity or obligation — what 'ought to be done' (amandus 'to be loved, deserving to be loved'). Unlike the gerund (a noun), the gerundive is a true adjective and agrees with its noun in gender, number, and case. It is central to the passive periphrastic construction.
notes: in-situ note deferred: these infinite verb-forms await a unified orienting treatment of the finite/infinite distinction (Pharr's typology, §116–117) and the place of the verbal nouns/adjectives within it, to be inserted at the start of the Conjugation section in the HTML; a per-term in-situ gloss at the shared §116/§117 anchor is awkward and would duplicate that treatment
in_situ_rationale: deferred to planned §116 finite/infinite treatment (see notes)

definition: The supine is a rare verbal noun of the fourth declension, surviving in just two case forms. The accusative in -um expresses purpose after a verb of motion; the ablative in -ū is used with certain adjectives. Its participial stem also helps form other parts of the verb.
notes: used but not defined by Pharr; in-situ note deferred: these infinite verb-forms await a unified orienting treatment of the finite/infinite distinction (Pharr's typology, §116–117) and the place of the verbal nouns/adjectives within it, to be inserted at the start of the Conjugation section in the HTML; a per-term in-situ gloss at the shared §116/§117 anchor is awkward and would duplicate that treatment
in_situ_rationale: deferred to planned §116 finite/infinite treatment (see notes)

definition: From the present, perfect, and perfect passive participial stems all forms of the verb can be derived; these are the principal parts

The first conjugation is the first of Latin's four verb families, made up of verbs whose present stem ends in -ā, with the present infinitive in -āre (amō, amāre 'to love'). It is the largest and most regular conjugation. Each conjugation shares a characteristic set of endings.
in_situ_rationale: member-of-a-class: §126 lays out what the four conjugations are and the differentiating axis (present-stem vowel, found by dropping -re), so the member is salient from the parent schema

definition: The second conjugation is the family of Latin verbs whose present stem ends in -ē, with the present infinitive in -ēre and a long e (moneō, monēre 'to warn'). The long ē distinguishes it from the third conjugation, whose infinitive ends in a short -ere.
in_situ_rationale: member-of-a-class: located by the §126 schema; §141 supplies its own paradigm

definition: The third conjugation is the family of Latin verbs whose present stem ends in a consonant or short -e, with the present infinitive in a short -ere (regō, regere 'to rule'). It includes a sizable -iō subgroup (capiō, capere) that borrows some endings from the fourth conjugation.
in_situ_rationale: member-of-a-class: located by the §126 schema; §151 supplies its own paradigm and the -iō subgroup

definition: The fourth conjugation is the family of Latin verbs whose present stem ends in -ī, with the present infinitive in -īre (audiō, audīre 'to hear').
in_situ_rationale: member-of-a-class: located by the §126 schema; §171 supplies its own paradigm

definition: Deponent verbs have passive form but active meaning
The periphrastic conjugation arises from combining the future active participle (or the gerundive) with forms of sum

variants: syncopated; syncope; contracted; contracted perfects; contracted verb forms
definition: forms with the tense-sign syllable dropped are called syncopated forms; they occur in all periods, and the poets use them to suit the meter
notes: the index's labels 'contracted perfects/verb forms' (§204) name the same phenomenon; merged from the former standalone 'contracted forms' gap entry in Thread 2

definition: A semideponent verb is one that behaves normally (active in form) in its present system, but switches to deponent behavior — passive in form, active in meaning — in its perfect system. Thus audeō 'I dare' is ordinary, but its perfect is ausus sum 'I dared,' passive in form yet active in sense.
in_situ_rationale: clear from context: §205 lists the verbs and shows the active-present/deponent-perfect pattern by example, so the meaning is self-evident in situ

definition: An irregular verb is one that does not follow the regular pattern of any of the four conjugations, but forms some of its parts in unpredictable ways that must be learned individually. The common Latin examples include sum 'be', possum 'be able', volō 'wish', ferō 'carry', eō 'go', and fīō 'become'.
in_situ_rationale: familiarity: clear from the everyday meaning of the English word 'irregular'; §206ff supply the individual verbs

definition: A verb agrees with its subject in number and person; an adjective agrees with its noun in gender, number, and case; a relative pronoun agrees with its antecedent in gender, number, and person
notes: principle stated across §236 ff.

definition: The predicate is the part of a sentence that states what is said about the subject. At its core it is the verb, together with any words that complete or modify it. In rēx urbem videt, 'the king sees the city,' the subject is rēx and the predicate is urbem videt.
notes: Pharr uses 'predicate' in two senses: the general sense (what is said about the subject; the verb) at §236a, §258; and the narrower 'predicate noun' family at §236c, §239, §240, §278, §285, §317 — split into a separate 'predicate noun' entry in Thread 2
in_situ_note: everything the sentence says about the subject, here the verb
in_situ_rationale: keep, appositive: §236a calls the verb 'the predicate' in the general subject/predicate sense; a student needs to know why the verb is so named. Note clarifies the general sense at first appearance; the narrower 'predicate noun' sense (§236c and later) is handled by its own entry || Thread 3: anchored at §236a ('may take its predicate'), the first occurrence of the term; appositive.

variants: predicate substantive; predicate attribute; predicate nominative
definition: A predicate noun is a noun (or other substantive) in the predicate that is joined to the subject by a linking verb — a copula such as est 'is', or a verb of seeming, becoming, or being called. It refers to the same person or thing as the subject and stands in the same case, the nominative: <<ille vir erat dux>>, 'that man was leader.' When the linked word is an adjective, Pharr calls it a [[predicate attribute]].
notes: first appears at §236c ('the predicate noun'); recurs as 'predicate substantive' (§240), 'predicate attribute' (§239, extending to adjectives), and in §278, §285, §317; new entry split from 'predicate' in Thread 2
in_situ_note: a noun joined to the subject by a linking verb such as est, standing in the same case
in_situ_rationale: keep, appositive: §236c introduces the narrower sense, naming the copula in passing; the gloss at first appearance carries forward to the later variant-labels (substantive, attribute, §240ff) || Thread 3: per instruction, appended to the plain-text 'predicate noun' in §236c (no .term anchor exists); appositive.

definition: the noun that a relative pronoun refers back to and agrees with in gender, number, and person
notes: used/glossed, not formally defined

definition: The infinitive is used after verbs of saying, showing, believing, and perceiving to express an indirect statement
notes: Pharr defines by construction

definition: An impersonal verb is one used only in the third-person singular, with no specific personal subject — English renders it with 'it' (pluit 'it rains', licet 'it is allowed'). Any person involved appears in an oblique case rather than as the subject.
in_situ_note: verbs used only in the third-person singular with no real subject, like English 'it rains' or 'it is allowed'
in_situ_rationale: keep, meaning-only: the English 'it'-verb analogy makes it clear; §290's construction (accusative/genitive) is Pharr's to give || Thread 3: anchored at §290 ('Impersonal verbs of emotion'); pluralized appositive. || Thread 3 revision: parenthetical placed immediately after 'verbs' (before 'of emotion'), since 'of emotion' is a specific subclass not being defined here.

definition: Primary tenses are ordinarily followed by primary tenses, secondary by secondary
Quod (the fact that, in that) is used with the indicative to introduce explanatory clauses after verbs of adding and dropping, doing and happening

A causal clause is a subordinate clause that states the reason or cause for the action of the main clause — answering 'why?' In Latin it is introduced by quod, quia, quoniam, or quandō, with the indicative when the speaker vouches for the reason as fact, the subjunctive when the reason is reported as someone else's.
notes: §357 gives the construction (quod/quia/quoniam/quandō + indic. or subj. to express cause)
in_situ_note: A causal clause gives the reason or cause for the main clause — the 'because' clause.
in_situ_rationale: keep, meaning-only: the 'because' sense is what the student needs to read §357; the quod/quia construction and mood rule are Pharr's to give || Thread 3: heading-anchored; added as a full intro sentence after the 'Causal Clauses' h4 (§357).

definition: Verbs of hindering are verbs meaning to prevent, hinder, refuse, or stand in the way (such as impediō, prohibeō, obstō, recūsō). In Latin they govern a subordinate clause with the subjunctive introduced by nē or quōminus (or, after a negative, quīn).
in_situ_note: These are verbs meaning to prevent, hinder, refuse, or stand in the way.
in_situ_rationale: keep, meaning-only (corrected framing): the note identifies the verb class the student needs; §361 supplies the nē/quōminus construction || Thread 3: heading-anchored; added as a full intro sentence after the 'Verbs of Hindering' h4 (§361).

definition: Verbs of fearing are verbs meaning to fear, dread, or be afraid (such as timeō, metuō, vereor). They take a subjunctive clause whose conjunction works in reverse of English expectation: nē means 'that' / 'lest' (the feared thing happening), and ut (or nē nōn) means 'that... not' (the feared thing failing to happen).
in_situ_note: These are verbs meaning to fear, dread, or be afraid.
in_situ_rationale: keep, meaning-only (corrected framing): the note supplies the verb class; the reversed nē/ut construction is Pharr's to give in §363 || Thread 3: heading-anchored; added as a full intro sentence after the 'Verbs of Fearing' h4 (§363).

definition: A temporal clause is a subordinate clause that tells when the action of the main clause happens — 'when', 'after', 'before', 'as soon as', 'while.' Latin introduces it with conjunctions such as ubi, postquam, cum, and dum, the mood and tense depending on the time relation.
notes: §368 gives the construction (ut/cum/ubi/postquam + tense rules)
in_situ_note: A temporal clause tells when the main action happens.
in_situ_rationale: keep, meaning-only: the 'when'-clause sense is the need; §368 supplies the conjunctions and the mood/tense rules || Thread 3: heading-anchored; added as a full intro sentence after the 'Temporal Clauses' h4 (§368).

definition: A cum clause is a subordinate clause introduced by the conjunction cum (not the preposition cum 'with'). With the indicative it is purely temporal ('when'); with the subjunctive it usually gives circumstance, cause, or concession ('since', 'although', 'when in fact'), the mood signaling which sense is meant.
notes: §377 gives the construction (temporal cum with indicative; circumstantial/causal cum with subjunctive)
in_situ_rationale: salience: self-evident — the clause is plainly the one introduced by cum, and §377 supplies the indicative/subjunctive sense distinction

definition: A conditional sentence states that one thing is or would be true if another is — an 'if... then...' sentence. Its two parts are the protasis, the 'if' clause stating the condition, and the apodosis, the main clause stating the result. Latin varies the mood and tense to show whether the condition is treated as factual, possible, or contrary to fact.
notes: §380 ff. classify types (logical/ideal/contrary-to-fact) by construction
in_situ_note: A conditional sentence is an 'if… then…' sentence — an 'if' clause (the protasis) stating a condition and a main clause (the apodosis) stating the result.
in_situ_rationale: keep, meaning-only: the if/then frame is the need; §380 introduces protasis/apodosis and the factual/possible/contrary-to-fact types itself || Thread 3: inline appositive after the bold .term in §380. || Thread 3 revision: moved from an inline appositive on the §380 .term to a full intro sentence after the 'Conditional Sentences' h4 heading, matching the other heading-anchored clause definitions; rephrased accordingly.

definition: the condition (protasis) of a conditional sentence
notes: glossed inline

definition: the conclusion (apodosis) of a conditional sentence
notes: glossed inline

definition: A concessive clause is a subordinate clause that grants a point that might seem to work against the main clause — 'although', 'even though', 'granted that.' Latin introduces it with conjunctions such as quamquam, etsī, etiamsī, and quamvīs, the mood depending on the conjunction.
notes: §384 gives the construction (etsī/quamquam/quamvīs + mood rules)
in_situ_note: a subordinate clause conceding a contrasting point — the 'although' clause
in_situ_rationale: keep, meaning-only: the 'although' sense is the need; §384 supplies the conjunctions and mood rules || Thread 3: inline appositive after the existing .term anchor in §384.

definition: A relative clause is a subordinate clause introduced by a relative pronoun (quī, quae, quod) that functions as an adjective, modifying a noun in the main sentence (its antecedent). In amō virum quī fortis est, 'I love the man who is brave,' the clause quī fortis est describes virum.
notes: reclassified Pharr->editor in Thread 2: Pharr's §385 gives a characteristic ('regularly has its verb in the indicative'), not a definition; the only definitional content is the parenthetical 'used as an adjective to modify a noun', now folded into the editor definition
in_situ_rationale: member/salience: 'relative pronoun' is glossed at §109 and the antecedent idea travels with it; by §385 the relative clause is recoverable from that gloss plus Pharr's own parenthetical 'used as an adjective to modify a noun', so no separate in-situ note

definition: The dactylic hexameter has six feet; the first five are dactyls or spondees, the sixth always a spondee (or trochee), and it was the meter of epic poetry
notes: defined across §391–392

definition: a spondee is two long syllables
a dactyl is one long syllable followed by two shorts
Scansion is the process of analyzing a line of verse to mark its rhythm — determining whether each syllable is long or short, dividing the line into its feet, and noting the metrical pattern.
notes: Pharr never defines scansion; §404 only demonstrates the procedure and notation (long bar / breve marks)
in_situ_note: the analysis of a verse line to mark its rhythm, finding which syllables are long or short and dividing the line into feet
in_situ_rationale: keep, meaning-only: salient procedure name; the long/short marks and 'foot' are present in the surrounding §392-404, so the meaning is the need || Thread 3: moved from §394 to §393, the first use of the word 'scansion'; appositive.

definition: a short final syllable standing in a verse position that is counted long in scansion
notes: Pharr describes the phenomenon (§394) rather than formally defining the term

definition: The final syllable of a hexameter verse may be either long or short (syllaba anceps), but in scansion is counted long
when a word ends with a vowel, diphthong, or m and the following word begins with a vowel or h, the first vowel or diphthong is regularly elided
when a word ending in a vowel, diphthong, or m is followed by a word beginning with a vowel or h and elision does not take place
Semi-hiatus: sometimes when a word ends in a long vowel or diphthong and the next word begins with a vowel or h, the long final vowel or diphthong is shortened; this is called semi-hiatus
notes: absent from extraction database; added in Thread 2; defined by Pharr at §400, parallel to hiatus (§399)

definition: when the final syllable of a verse is elided before a vowel beginning the next verse
Synizesis: two successive vowels that do not ordinarily form a diphthong are sometimes pronounced as one syllable for the meter; this contraction within a word is called synizesis
notes: Pharr definition at §403 missed in extraction (defined at first appearance)

definition: a verse whose final syllable is elided into the next is a hypermeter or hypermetric verse
Caesura: whenever a word ends within a foot, the break is called caesura
Antithesis is an opposition or contrast of words or ideas

A conjunction is a word that joins other words, phrases, or clauses together (et 'and', sed 'but', aut 'or', quod 'because'). Some conjunctions link items of equal weight; others attach a subordinate clause to a main one and, in Latin, often govern the mood of that clause's verb.
in_situ_rationale: familiarity / salience: at §425 'conjunction' appears only inside the definition of hendiadys, where it is incidental and clear from the example

definition: An interjection is a short exclamatory word that expresses feeling and stands grammatically apart from the rest of the sentence (ō 'oh!', heu 'alas!', ēn 'look!'). It has no case, tense, or other inflection.
in_situ_rationale: familiarity / salience: appears only in the index here; an everyday notion ('oh!', 'alas!') needing no in-situ gloss

definition: A defective verb is one that lacks some of the forms a complete verb would have — missing certain tenses, persons, or moods. Some Latin verbs exist only in their perfect-system forms yet carry present meaning (coepī 'I begin', meminī 'I remember', ōdī 'I hate').
in_situ_note: one missing some of the forms a complete verb would have
in_situ_rationale: keep, meaning-only: the 'incomplete set of forms' idea is the need; the perfect-with-present-meaning examples left to context || SKIPPED in Thread 3: Pharr does not use 'defective' terminology; §447 was an index artifact. The cited passage (§205) treats these as semi-deponents. No gloss inserted.

definition: Apposition is the placing of one noun beside another that refers to the same thing, to identify or describe it; the two nouns stand in the same case. In urbs Rōma, 'the city Rome,' Rōma is in apposition to urbs.
in_situ_note: a noun set beside another that names the same thing, standing in the same case
in_situ_rationale: keep, meaning-only: 'case' is foundational and long established; the meaning is what the student needs, the example left to context || Thread 3: moved from §447 (index artifact) to §241, where 'appositive' appears in plain text; reworded to fit that context.

definition: A purpose clause is a subordinate clause that tells the aim or intention behind the main action — 'in order that', 'so that', 'to.' Latin expresses it with the subjunctive introduced by ut (positive) or nē (negative).
notes: §359 gives the construction (present/imperfect subjunctive with ut or nē), not a definition
in_situ_note: a subordinate clause telling the aim or intention behind the main action — 'in order that', 'so that'
in_situ_rationale: keep, meaning-only: the 'in order to' sense distinguishes it from result; §359 supplies the ut/nē + subjunctive construction || SKIPPED in Thread 3: already well explained in the body text (purpose construction at §359); §447 was an index artifact, not a real anchor. No gloss inserted.

definition: A result clause is a subordinate clause that states the consequence or effect of the main action — 'so... that', 'with the result that.' Latin expresses it with the subjunctive introduced by ut (positive) or ut nōn (negative), often signaled by a word such as tam, sīc, or tantus in the main clause.
notes: §364 gives the construction (subjunctive with ut or ut nōn denotes result), not a definition
in_situ_note: a subordinate clause stating the consequence or effect of the main action — 'so... that'
in_situ_rationale: keep, meaning-only: the 'so...that' sense is what separates it from purpose; §364 supplies the ut / ut nōn construction and the signal words || SKIPPED in Thread 3: already well explained in the body text (result construction at §364); §447 was an index artifact, not a real anchor. No gloss inserted.

definition: Alliteration is the repetition of the same letter or sound
Anaphora is the repetition of a word or words at the beginning of successive clauses
Anastrophe is an inversion of the usual order of words
Aposiopesis is a breaking off before the close of the sentence
Apostrophe is a sudden break from the previous method of discourse to address, in the second person, some person or object, absent or present
Asyndeton is the omission of conjunctions
Brachylogy is the failure to repeat an element that must be supplied in modified form
Chiasmus is the arrangement of corresponding pairs of words in opposite order
Ellipsis is the omission of words obviously understood but needed to complete the construction
Enallage is a shift from one form to another
Euphemism is the substitution of an agreeable word for one that is harsh or unpleasant
Euphony is the effect produced by words or sounds so combined as to please the ear
Hendiadys is the expression of one idea by two nouns joined by a conjunction, instead of a noun with a modifier
Hypallage is an interchange in the relations of words
Hyperbaton is the violent displacement of words
Hyperbole is rhetorical exaggeration
Hysteron Proteron is a reversal of the natural order
Irony states an apparent fact with the manifest intention of expressing its opposite
Litotes is an expression by which more is meant than meets the ear, especially with the negative
Metaphor is an implied comparison
Metonymy is the substitution of one word for another it suggests
Onomatopoeia is the use of words whose sound suggests the sense
Oxymoron is the use of words apparently contradictory
Paraleipsis is an apparent omission by which a speaker artfully pretends to pass by what he really emphasizes
Personification attributes personality to an impersonal thing
Pleonasm is the use of superfluous words
Polysyndeton is the use of unnecessary conjunctions
Prolepsis is the use of a word, usually a modifier, before it is logically appropriate
Simile is a figure that asserts an explicit comparison between two different things
Synchysis is an interlocked order of words
Synecdoche is the use of a part for the whole, or the reverse
Synesis is a construction according to sense rather than grammatical form
Tmesis is the separation of the parts of a compound word by intervening words
Transferred Epithet is an epithet transferred from the word to which it strictly belongs to another word connected with it in thought
Zeugma is the joining of two words with a modifying or governing word that strictly applies to only one of them
Anacoluthon (want of sequence) occurs when the scheme of a sentence is changed before it is completed
